coredata.dc:title: The effect of N-doped quantum dots on the properties of in situ prepared colorless polyimide nanocomposite films
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yong
Yang, Zenghui
Wang, Qihua
Wang, Tingmei
coredata.dc:description: Conventional polyimide nanocomposites reinforced with graphene-based nanofillers usually possess excellent properties, such as high thermal decomposition temperature, enhanced tensile strength, tensile modulus, etc. However, the elongation at break of the nanocomposites is reduced. Here, we prepared N-doped graphene quantum dots/colorless polyimide nanocomposite (N-GQDs/CPI) films for the first time by in-situ polymerization, whose elongation at break is about 2 times that of the pure film. Meanwhile, the thermal properties and other mechanical properties of the films are improved. Through analyzing FT-IR, XPS, XRD, TAG SEM, etc. of N-GQDs and the films, we consider that the strengthening mechanism of matrix performance is attributed to N-GQDs' well dispersion, its small size effects and strong interface interaction with the matrix. Furthermore, at the indicative wavelength of 550nm, the optical transmittance of the film with 1wt% N-GQDs still maintains 79%, indicating the resulting films have excellent optical transmittance. The hydrophilic properties of the prepared films' surface is reduced, their water surface contact angle are increased from 63±2.7° to 92±1.7° with the content of N-GQDs increasing. This research shows that N-GQDs can enhance the various properties of polyimide and provides a high performance nanocomposite films for potential flexible substrates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped graphene quantum dots
Polyimide nanocomposite films
Thermal properties
Mechanical properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films
Thermal properties of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films
Mechanical properties of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films
Optical performance, water surface contact angle of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The illustration of preparation process of N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films.
(a) TEM image of the obtained N-GQDs, inset shows the DMAc solution of N-GQDs in daylight (left) and 365nm UV lamp (right), respectively; (b) High-resolution TEM image of N-GQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs; (b) XPS full spectrum of N-GQDs; (c) and (d) XPS high-resolution spectrum of C 1s and N 1s.
(a) FT-IR spectra of pure CPI and the N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films; (b) XRD spectra of N-GQDs, pure CPI and the nanocomposite films.

(a) The TAG curves of N-GQDs, pure CPI and the N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films; (b) The tanδ-T curves and (c) The storage modulus-T curves of pure CPI and the nanocomposite films; (d) UV–visible transmittance spectroscopy curves of the nanocomposite films.
(a) The tensile strength and tensile modulus and (b) the elongation at break of pure CPI and the N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films.
Strengthening mechanism of elongation at break.
(a) SEM image of pure CPI and (b) the N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films' cross-section.
Water surface contact angle of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films.
Thermal properties of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films.
Mechanical properties of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films.
Optical performance and water surface contact angle of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films.

head.ce:title: The effect of N-doped quantum dots on the properties of in situ prepared colorless polyimide nanocomposite films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100039, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional polyimide nanocomposites reinforced with graphene-based nanofillers usually possess excellent properties, such as high thermal decomposition temperature, enhanced tensile strength, tensile modulus, etc. However, the elongation at break of the nanocomposites is reduced. Here, we prepared N-doped graphene quantum dots/colorless polyimide nanocomposite (N-GQDs/CPI) films for the first time by in-situ polymerization, whose elongation at break is about 2 times that of the pure film. Meanwhile, the thermal properties and other mechanical properties of the films are improved. Through analyzing FT-IR, XPS, XRD, TAG SEM, etc. of N-GQDs and the films, we consider that the strengthening mechanism of matrix performance is attributed to N-GQDs' well dispersion, its small size effects and strong interface interaction with the matrix. Furthermore, at the indicative wavelength of 550nm, the optical transmittance of the film with 1wt% N-GQDs still maintains 79%, indicating the resulting films have excellent optical transmittance. The hydrophilic properties of the prepared films' surface is reduced, their water surface contact angle are increased from 63±2.7° to 92±1.7° with the content of N-GQDs increasing. This research shows that N-GQDs can enhance the various properties of polyimide and provides a high performance nanocomposite films for potential flexible substrates.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyimides, a class of engineering plastics with excellent mechanical properties, thermal stability, electrical performance and low weight, have been applied to aerospace, microelectronic devices, automotive and other fields [1–3]. The applications of typical polyimides, however, are limited when they are used as substrates or interlayer material in the advanced flexible electronic devices, owing to their inherent coloration caused by the intra and intermolecular charge transfer interactions of the polyimide backbones [1]. Therefore, to solve the limitations of polyimide films on the advanced flexible electronic devices, the flexible CPI films with high thermal stability and mechanical properties are widely researched. Up to now, CPI mainly are synthesized by introducing some substituents or structures with high electronegativity, such as trifluoromethyl (CF3), sulfone (SO2 ), ether (O) or alicyclic structure (cyclohexane) to the imide main chain [2,4,5], because these substituents or structures can prohibit effectively intra and intermolecular charge transfer interactions of the polyimide backbones. However, thermal stability and mechanical properties of the CPI containing these substituents or structures are dramatically decreased due to the bulky structure and poor packing order [6–8].

In the past few years, graphene-based nanofillers have attracted extensive research interest in many fields, such as optoelectronics, bio-applications, and functional materials. Wei et al. [9] reported a hybrid cuprous oxide/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites, which can endow the fabricated devices with a relatively high switching ratio of 3.25 and a fast response time of 0.45s. Zang et al. [10] reported the water-soluble AgInZnS–graphene oxide nanocomposites with tunable emissions, which can be used as an active biomarker for noninvasive biomedical imaging. Yang et al. [11] reported the shape memory graphene-vitrimer composites with undetected strain shift and constant shape fixity and recovery values (Rf >99%, Rr >98%), what's more interesting was that the shape of the composites can be randomly changed via dynamic covalent transesterification reaction. Meanwhile, based on the intrinsic high Young's modulus, strength and excellent thermal conductivity of graphene, graphene-based nanofillers are widely used to improve the mechanical and thermal properties of polyimides. Cao et al. [12] prepared the polyimide nanocomposite films filled with (3-aminopropyl)trimethoxysilane-functionalized reduced graphene oxide, finding the tensile strength and Young's modulus of composites with 0.3wt% nanofillers were 31% and 35% higher than that of pure polyimide, respectively. Huang et al. [13] reported the polyimide composite with 1wt% chemically modified graphene, whose tensile strength and Young's modulus were improved by 30% and 46%, respectively. Chen et al. [14] synthesized the graphene fluoroxide/polyimide films composites, and the glass transition temperature (Tg) of the composite containing 0.2wt% fillers was increased from 355°C for pure polyimide to 388°C. Lu et al. [15] achieved 13% increase in tensile strength and ~58% increase in Young's modulus for polyimide nanocomposite containing 1wt% graphene oxide, compared with pure polyimide. Although the mechanical properties and thermal properties of the polyimide-based nanocomposites mentioned above have been significantly improved, their elongation at break is reduced. In other word, the obtained nanocomposite films could not be able to be applied to advanced flexible electronics. It is necessary to seek another nanofiller that can comprehensively strengthen the mechanical and thermal properties of matrix materials.

As is known to all, when the content of nanoparticles in the polymer matrix is fixed, the decrease of nanoparticles' size will increase the volume fraction of the interface layer between it and matrix [16], this may endow the resulting nanocomposites with different properties compared with that of nanocomposites enhanced by the usual size nanoparticles. Cheng et al. [16] reported a polymer nanocomposites filled with small (diameter~1.8nm) nanoparticles, finding the obtained nanocomposites exhibited zero-shear viscosity comparable to that of pure polymer at high temperatures and the tunable range of their Tg and fragility far exceeds that of conventional polymer nanocomposites. Xia et al. [17] prepared the polyimide nanocomposites containing SiO2 particles with a particle size of <10nm, discovering the interfaces between the SiO2 particles and the polyimide matrix may greatly impact the electrical properties of nanocomposite films. Graphene quantum dots, which have been extensively applied in photocatalyst, energy conversion, sensor and bioimaging duo to their unique chemical, electronic and optical properties [18–21], are a class of nanoparticles with a much smaller particle size than graphene. Simultaneously, graphene quantum dots, as a member of the graphene-based nanomaterials, have a structure similar to graphene. Therefore, graphene quantum dots have potential in improving the properties of polymer composites. To our best knowledge, there are very few reports using graphene quantum dots to improve the performance of polyimide matrix until now. For the above reasons, we prepared the N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films by in situ synthesis method and investigated the thermal, mechanical and other properties of the obtained nanocomposite films in detail. The results demonstrate that the prepared nanocomposite films possess outstanding thermal and mechanical properties, especially the elongation at break is significantly improved compared to that of CPI films. At the same time, the optical transmittance of the prepared nanocomposite films is not markedly reduced. Hence, the obtained nanocomposite films are expected to be used as transparent thin-film materials for flexible display substrates.

4,4′-(Hexafluoroisopropylidene) diphthalic anhydride (6FDA) was purchased from Changzhou Sunlight Medical RawMaterial Co., Ltd. (China). 2,2′-Bis(triuoromethyl) benzidine (TFDB) was bought from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing). N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc) was obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagents Co. (China). Urea was procured from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (China). Citric acid monohydrate was acquired from Tianjin Ke Miou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All chemicals are commercial available and analysis pure grade, and they were used without further purification. Dialysis membranes were received from Shanghai source Leaf Biological Technology Co., Ltd.

N-GQDs were prepared according to previous reports [22,23]. 1.05g citric acid monohydrate and 0.9g urea were dissolved into 25ml deionized water and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 100ml Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was placed in an electric oven at 200°C and maintained 6h. Subsequently, the obtained brown liquid was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min to remove larger particles. The supernatant was dialyzed against pure water through a dialysis membrane (Da=1000) for 24h to remove unreacted molecules and small particles. The dialyzed solution was then concentrated and solvent exchanged with DMAc in Rotary evaporator. The concentration of the obtained DMAc solution of N-GQDs was 0.0023g/ml.

The methods of in situ polymerization and thermal imidization were used to prepare N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films containing various concentrations of N-GQDs. A typical procedure of the nanocomposite film with 0.5wt% N-GQDs is as follows: 0.5ml 0.0023g/ml DMAc solution of N-GQDs was poured into 250ml three-neck flask containing 19.5ml pure DMAc and sonicated for 2h (the total content of DMAc remained at 20ml). Then, install a mechanical stirrer and nitrogen inlet device for the three-neck flask. TFDB (0.960g, 3mmol) was added into the three-neck flask and stirred for 1h under ice-water bath. 6FDA (1.332g, 3mmol) was mixed with the above solution in the three-neck flask and reacted for 14h. The whole reaction process was in the ice-water bath and nitrogen atmosphere. The obtained mixture was poured on a clear glass substrate and imidized in an electric oven following the procedure below: at 80°C for 4h, and at 200, 260, 300°C for 1h. After the film was cooled to room temperature naturally, the glass substrate was immersed into hot water and then the film was peeled off from the glass, the thickness of obtained films is about 70μm and the content of N-GQDs in the films is 0.1wt%, 0.25wt%, 0.5wt%, 0.75wt% and 1wt%, respectively. The illustration of preparation process of nanocomposite films is presented in Fig. 1 .

High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN) was applied to observe the morphology of N-GQDs. The microstructure of cross-sectional surface of the films was surveyed by using a SU8020 ultra-high resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA) was performed under nitrogen on a Netzsch-STA449F3 simultaneous thermal analysis system using a heating rate of 10°Cmin−1 from 18 to 800°C. The mechanical performance was measured by an electrical universal material testing machine with a 500N load cell (EZ-Test, SHIMADZU), the crosshead movement rate was 1mm/min, and the final data for each sample was averaged value after six measurements. FT-IR spectra was recorded on a Nexus 870 instrument by using the attenuated total reflectance (ATR) method. The wide-angle X-ray diffraction measurements of the films was characterized using powder X-ray diffraction (Rigaku D/MAX–2400 X-ray diffractometer with Ni-filtered Cu Ka radiation). Scanning range was from 10 to 80°. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (Thermon Scientific, ESCALAB250xi spectrometer) was used to analyze the chemical structure of element of N-GQDs. The dynamic mechanical properties of the films was measured utilizing dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA, Netzsch, DMA 242C) at a heating rate of 5°C/min from 30°C to 400°C. The DSA100 contact angle meter was employed to investigate water surface contact angle of the films, and the last data was the average value of five duplicate measurements. The transmittance spectrum of the films was recorded using the Perkin-Elmer Lambda 8100c spectrometer.

The obtained N-GQDs are investigated by TEM. As show in Fig. 2a, the size about 5nm N-GQDs can be clearly observed and their distribution is very homogeneous. High resolution TEM (HR-TEM) (Fig. 2b) clearly shows the 0.24nm lattice parameter of N-GQDs, which corresponds to the (1120) crystal phase of graphene [24]. The figure inserted Fig. 2a is the DMAc solution of N-GQDs in daylight (left) and 365nm UV lamp (right), respectively. It is clearly observed that light blue light from N-GQDs in 365nm UV lamp. These phenomena illustrate the successful synthesis of N-GQDs.

The surface composition of the prepared N-GQDs is characterized by FT-IR and XPS. The FT-IR feature absorption peaks of N-GQDs can be distinctly observed in the Fig. 3a. The peak located at 3531cm−1 is the stretching vibration of OH. The peaks situated at 3480, 3281cm−1 and 3012, 2932cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of NH2 and CH, respectively. Meanwhile, the stretching vibration absorption peaks of COOH and CONH appear at 1747cm−1 and 1635cm−1. And the characteristic peak of 1549cm−1 is from the bending vibration of CC bond [22,25]. As shown in Fig. 3b. The full scan XPS spectrogram of N-GQDs shows three main peaks originating from C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s, which suggests that the N-GQDs consist of elements C, N and O. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s in Fig. 3c is made up of three peaks at 284.5, 285.4 and 288.5eV, which correspond to CC/C-C, CN, and CO/CN, respectively [26,27]. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1s in Fig. 3d exhibits three peaks at 399.1, 400.1 and 400.8eV, which are attributed to the pyridine N, graphitic N and pyrrolic N/NH, respectively [28]. The results of FT-IR and XPS spectra of N-GQDs illustrate that there exist plenty of oxygen-containing functional groups, nitrogen-containing functional groups and nitrogen in different chemical environments on of N-GQDs. These special functional groups and structures enable N-GQDs to be easily soluble in DMAc and are beneficial to the interaction between polyamide acid and N-GQDs in the in situ polymerization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra is used to investigate the extent of imidization of pure CPI and the obtained nanocomposite films and the interaction of N-GQDs with the polyimide molecular chains. As shown in Fig. 4a, the characteristic absorption peaks of the imide group appeared at around 1787, 1730cm−1 and 1367cm−1, which are assigned to CO asymmetrical, symmetrical stretching vibration and CN stretching vibration, respectively. And the typical absorption peak of CO stretching vibration of the amide group located at approximate 1660cm−1 do not appear on the FT-IR spectra of the obtained nanocomposite films. These evidences indicate strongly the presence of the imide group and completion of imidization [15,29]. There are no distinctly, meanwhile, observable difference at FT-IR absorption spectra of pure CPI film and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films before wavenumber 2000cm−1, which probably is resulted from the too low amount of N-GQDs. However, compared with FT-IR spectra of pure PI, the two feeble peaks at 2924cm−1 and 2855cm−1 appear at the FT-IR spectra of the obtained nanocomposite films, which may be ascribed to the stretching vibration of Caliph H on N-GQDs [30–32], demonstrating the successful introduction of N-GQDs to polyimide matrix. The XRD patterns of N-GQDs, pure CPI film and the obtained nanocomposite films are presented at Fig. 4b. It is clearly observed that the N-GQDs have a relatively wide peak centered at around 2θ=20°, which is located at the lower degree compared with 2θ=29° of graphene [33]. This indicates the N-GQDs have a bigger interlayer spacing than graphene, which was caused by oxygen-containing groups and nitrogen-containing groups between layers of N-GQDs [24]. The result is consistent with the conclusion of the FT-IR spectra and XPS of N-GQDs. For the pure CPI film and its nanocomposite films, there are a broad peak at around 2θ=16°, indicating that the prepared films are amorphous. Furthermore, with the increase of the amount of N-GQDs introduced into the films, no distinct difference was observed from the XRD spectra of the films, demonstrating N-GQDs in matrix are enough exfoliated state and well dispersed in matrix [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal properties of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The thermal properties is one of the significant performance of polyimide-based nanocomposites as a potential engineering plastics for practical application. Fig. 5a shows the TAG curves of N-GQDs, pure CPI and the obtained nanocomposite films, respectively. In terms of N-GQDs, it is easily found that the quality of N-GQDs has a drastic decrease at 170°C owing to the degradation of the functional groups [23]. Whereas, after N-GQDs are incorporated into the polyimide matrix, there is almost no changes at the initial degradation temperature of the obtained nanocomposite films, indicating N-GQDs successfully are dispersed into polyimide matrix and the polymer chains play a protective role against the degradation of N-GQDs by the interaction of covalent bond and strong hydrogen bonding between the polymer chains and N-GQDs. In addition, when the content of N-GQDs in polyimide matrix achieves 0.25wt%, the temperature for 5% and 10% weight loss of the resulting nanocomposites is improved from 526°C and 546°C for pure CPI film to 532°C and 552°C, respectively. After, the thermal stability of the films decreases slightly with the content of N-GQDs increasing. The changing trend of thermal degradation temperature of the nanocomposite films can be clearly observed in the illustration in Fig. 5a. In general, the thermal stability of the resulting nanocomposite materials is improved after the nanofiller is introduced into the polymer matrix [14,34–36], and for N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films, the improvement in thermal stability can be explained by the below reasons: first of all, the carbon surface of N-GQDs in the matrix might serve as a radical scavenger, which can effectively delay the degradation temperature [34]. Next, the incorporation of N-GQDs into the polyimide matrix can achieve the “tortuous path” effect, which availably prevents the permeation of oxygen and the overflow of volatile degradation products in the matrix [37]. Furthermore, the improved thermal stability is related with the strong interface interaction between N-GQDs and matrix [15].

The tanδ-T curves of pure CPI and the obtained nanocomposite films are illustrated in Fig. 5b. It is clearly observed that the tanδ peaks of the films move toward a higher temperature with the addition of N-GQDs to the polymer matrix, indicating that Tg of polyimide matrix is heightened. Compared with pure CPI film, Tg of the nanocomposite films with a low content of N-GQDs (0.1wt% and 0.25wt%) are significantly increased, where the improvement values of 12°C and 11°C are achieved, respectively. The enhancement is caused by immobilization of polymer chains near the N-GQDs surface. In other word, N-GQDs possess well dispersion in the polyimide matrix and oxygen-containing groups and nitrogen-containing groups on its surface might form covalent bond as well as strong hydrogen bonding with matrix. These two features availably hinder the movement of polymer chains around N-GQDs surface, causing the enhancement of the nanocomposite films' Tg. While, Tg of the nanocomposite films is slightly decreased with the further increase of N-GQDs content, illustrating the low content of N-GQDs is more useful for the improvement of Tg of the composites duo to better dispersion in polyimide matrix. Fig. 5c shows the functional relationship between the storage modulus of the nanocomposite films and the temperature. Before 150°C, the storage modulus of the films is significantly changed with the introduction of quantum dots. And the storage modulus of the nanocomposite film with 0.75wt% N-GQDs achieves 3.68GPa and is 1.28 times that of pure CPI film at 150°C. The improved storage modulus can be ascribed to well dispersion of N-GQDs in matrix and the restriction of N-GQDs on the movement of polyimide chains. Afterwards, the films' storage modulus is reduced at 1wt%N-GQDs, which may be caused by small agglomeration of N-GQDs. The detail datum are listed in Table 1 .

High dimensional stability is vital for polyimide-based nanocomposites as the substrate materials. Because the materials are usually laminated with other materials, followed by high-temperature processing. The coefficient of linear thermal expansion (CLTEs) datum of pure CPI and the nanocomposite films are showed in Table 1. The reducing trend from 31.6ppm/°C for pure CPI film to 26.1ppm/°C for the nanocomposite film with 0.5wt% N-GQDs can be readily observed, demonstrating N-GQDs is benefit for the CLTEs reduction of the nanocomposite films. And the reduction of CLTEs for the films containing N-GQDs is related with the restriction of N-GQDs on segmental mobility of polyimide chains, which is resulted from well dispersion of N-GQDs and the strong interfacial interaction between it with matrix [38]. While, the CLTEs of films are increased at 1wt% N-GQDs compared with the films containing the lower content quantum dots, illustrating that the reducing effect of N-GQDs on the CLTEs of films is decreased, which is ascribed to the partial agglomeration of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: Mechanical properties is an important parameter for polyimide nanocomposites. After the nanofiller is incorporated into the polymer matrix, the enhanced mechanical properties is the result that is expected to see. As showed in Fig. 6a. The tensile strength and tensile modulus of the obtained nanocomposite films are strengthened from about 90MPa and 2.42GPa for pure CPI film to 124MPa and 2.79GPa for the nanocomposite film with 1wt% N-GQDs. The approximate 38% and 15% increase in tensile strength and tensile modulus are achieved, respectively. The enhancement of mechanical performance is primarily attributed to following reasons: firstly, the N-GQDs have well dispersion in the polyimide matrix. For another, the strong interface interaction between polyimide matrix and N-GQDs can efficiently transfer the load from matrix to N-GQDs [29,39]. And in our system, although N-GQDs have a certain agglomeration at high content, the effective transfer of load is all achieved as the N-GQDs content increase from 0.1 to 1wt%. Hence, the tensile strength and tensile modulus of film with 1wt% N-GQDs have the most significant enhancement. The corresponding datum of tensile strength and modulus of the nanocomposite films are summarized in Table 2 . In generally, on account of the existence of the pair contradictory between strength and toughness in the properties of nanocomposites, the tensile strength and modulus of nanocomposites usually are enhanced after the addition of nanofiller, while their elongation at break is reduced. It is worth noting that the 91% improvement in elongation at break of the film with 0.75wt% N-GQDs is achieved. The markedly intensification of elongation at break of films clearly is seen from Fig. 6b. In order to exclude the influence of the elongation at break of pure CPI film on the experimental results, we compared elongation at break of the pure film prepared in this work with that in the literature [40]. It is shown that the results are similar. As we know, the biggest difference between graphene quantum dots and other graphene-based nanofillers comes from their size. Compared with other graphene-based nanofillers, the ~5nm size of graphene quantum dots allows it to have more contact areas with the polyimide matrix under certain amounts. Therefore, we firstly attribute the comprehensive improvement of the obtained nanocomposite films' mechanical properties to the small size effects of N-GQDs, which provides more contact area for N-GQDs and polyimide matrix. Secondly, It is learned from the above discussions that there are plenty of oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing groups on N-GQDs surface. This provide abundant reaction sites to construct strong interfacial interaction by hydrogen bonding and covalent bond between N-GQDs and polyimide matrix. At the same time, multiple different forms N (such as the pyridine N, graphitic N and pyrrolic N) in the surface of N-GQDs further strength the interaction between N-GQDs and polyimide matrix. Based on the two points mentioned above and the previous reported literatures [41,42], we consider that the mechanism of the comprehensive improvement of the nanocomposite films' mechanical properties is caused by the large contact area and strong interaction, which is ascribed to a bio-inspired toughening mechanism, mainly including two parts: sacrificial bonds and hidden length. When a load is applied to the obtained nanocomposite films, most of the applied load mainly is transferred from polymer chains of matrix to N-GQDs by the sacrificial bonds originated from hydrogen bonding. Further increasing of the load, the sacrificial bonds gradually rupture and the hidden length is released. Hence, the elongation at break of the nanocomposite films are significantly improved in the case where the tensile strength and modulus are improved. The strengthened mechanism of the elongation at break is illustrated in Fig. 7 . The partial agglomeration of nanoparticles can reduce the contact area between the polymer and nanofiller, causing an opposite response in mechanical properties [43]. Thus, the reduction of the elongation at break of the film with 1wt% N-GQDs may result from partial agglomeration of N-GQDs.

In order to further study the enhancement mechanism of N-GQDs on tensile strength, modulus and the elongation at break of the obtained nanocomposite films, we analyzed the cross-section of the nanocomposite film splines by using a SU8020 ultra-high resolution field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM). As revealed in Fig. 8 . After the introduction of N-GQDs into the polyimide matrix, the morphology of the nanocomposite films' cross section has been changed significantly. Compared with the rough and random fractured surface of pure CPI film (Fig. 8a), the more regular scaly appearance appears on the cross-section of the nanocomposite film with 0.75wt% N-GQDs (Fig. 8b). The regular scaly morphology is believed to be caused by the intense interactions between the fillers and polymer matrix [41], further confirming the mechanical performance improvement of the nanocomposite films is resulted from covalent bond and strong hydrogen bonding between N-GQDs with polyimide matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical performance, water surface contact angle of pure CPI and N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent optical transparency is an indispensable property for the polyimide films as flexible display substrates. Therefore, the Perkin-Elmer Lambda 8100c spectrometer is used to explore the optical performance of the obtained nanocomposite films. As revealed in Fig. 5d. With increasing N-GQDs content, although optical transmittance of the nanocomposite films gradually is reduced, at the indicative wavelength of 550nm, the optical transmittance of the nanocomposite film with 1wt% N-GQDs still remains 79% compared with 95% of pure CPI film. Meanwhile, there is no obvious changes in cut-off wavelength for pure CPI and the nanocomposite films. These all indicate the obtained nanocomposite films possess glorious optical transparency. The reason why the optical transmittance is not obviously damaged is attributed to the lower content of N-GQDs and the performance that they possess excellent optical transmittance (~98%) in a wide range of light wavelengths [44]. Simultaneously, compared with the optical transmittance of polyimide substrates (about 86% and 75%) in some reports [45,46], the 79% transmittance is in an acceptable range. Hence, the obtained nanocomposite films may be a transparent thin-film materials for flexible display substrates. The corresponding datum are presented in Table 3 . The water surface contact angle of the nanocomposite films is investigated to realize effect of N-GQDs on hydrophilic properties of its surface. The digital photos of water surface contact angle of pure CPI film and the obtained nanocomposite films are shown in Fig. 9 . It is distinctly observed that the water surface contact angle of nanocomposite films is increased gradually with increasing N-GQDs content, and the nanocomposite film with 1wt% N-GQDs achieves the maximal water surface contact angle of 93±1.7°. The reduction of hydrophilic properties for the obtained nanocomposite films could be caused by the fact that the oxygen-containing and nitrogen-containing functional groups of N-GQDs on the films' surface are removed during the process that films are cured at high temperature, causing that N-GQDs become hydrophobic and water droplet aggregate on the films' surface.

In this work, the N-GQDs/CPI nanocomposite films possessing high-performance were firstly prepared via in situ polymerization method. The mechanical and thermal properties of the obtained films are strengthened significantly. In particular, the elongation at break of the nanocomposite film with 0.75wt% N-GQDs is increased by 91% compared with that of pure CPI film. For other enhanced aspects, 38% and 15% increase in the tensile strength and tensile modulus are achieved at the film with 1wt% N-GQDs, respectively. Tg of the film with 0.1wt% N-GQDs is 12°C higher than that of pure CPI film. At the same time, CLTEs of the nanocomposite films is reduced from 31.6ppm/°C for the pure CPI film to 26.1ppm/°C for the film with 0.5wt% N-GQDs. The experimental results show the performance enhancement of nanocomposite films are due to N-GQDs' the well dispersion, its small size effects and strong interface interaction with the matrix. Furthermore, the optical transmittance of the obtained nanocomposite films after the addition of N-GQDs has the reducing trend, although the nanocomposite film with 1wt% N-GQDs still keep 79% optical transmittance at the indicative wavelength of 550nm, illustrating that the nanocomposite films have excellent optical transparency. And the hydrophilic properties of the obtained nanocomposite films' surface are reduced relative to that of the pure CPI film. The comprehensive improvement of the obtained nanocomposite films' performance enables it to be a potential candidate for flexible display substrates.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We greatly appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 51673205) and the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, Grant No. 2015CB057502).
